DraftPolisher is a tool for fast polishing of draft sequences. DraftPolisher can produce an improved consensus sequence for a draft genome assembly (or any draft sequence assembly) by using a reference genome and a reference database of sequences (by which the frequences of a defined k-mer, representative of each mismatch, will be evaluated) both in FASTA format.DraftPolisher is designed for the polishing of draft sequences. Two different releases of the tool are present: DraftPolisher.py and DraftPolisher_cov.py. The "cov" release (i.e. coverage release) takes into account also the coverage of the sequences from the reference database used for the polishing. The "cov" release was designed based on the SPAdes assembling output file format, where the coverage value is reported in the last part of the sequences IDs, so if using this version of the tool take care of the reference sequences file format (see SPAdes output format for more details).Three files are required by the tool: the query sequence to polish, a reference sequence, a reference database of sequences and the size of the k-mer to use to perform the polishing (we suggest k-mer size = 8). In "Test" folder data for testing the tool are available.
Underlying_algorithms: The tool performs a first alignment against a reference sequence (e.g. reference genome) and identify all the missmatches. After, each of the missmatches is evaluated considering a reference database of high quality sequences ( provided by the user). These could be whatever sequences in fasta format. The polishing of the draft sequence is performed considering the frequency of kmers of defined size (default is 16 bp) around each of the missmatches. if the frequency for the mismatches and the reference sequence is the same the missmach is kept, for a conservative approach.
Underlying_assumptions: The reference database of sequences provided, is assumed to be in average a good representation of the correct sequences. This tool is ment for short gaps or mismatches. The tool was designed to use SPAdes tool assembling output as reference database of sequences but can be easly adapted for other sequence database formats.
Strengths_weaknesses: The tool is very fast using MinION data but cannot be used with fastq files (only fasta)
Overall_performance: For MinION assembling data the tool requires few minutes to generate a better quality assembly

Underlying_algorithms: LAMSA takes takes the advantage of the rareness of SVs to implement a specifically designed two-step strategy. That is, LAMSA initially splits the read into relatively long fragments and co-linearly align them to solve the small variations or sequencing errors, and mitigate the effect of repeats. The alignments of the fragments are then used for implementing a sparse dynamic programming (SDP)-based split alignment approach to handle the large or non-co-linear variants.

Physlr constructs next-generation physical maps using linked read data (akin to physical maps used in the Human Genome Project but using next-gen. seq. data). Physlr then uses these physical maps to scaffold draft assemblies. The maps may be used for other genomics analyses such as variant calling, disassembly detection, phasing, etc.
Underlying_algorithms: Physlr builds an overlap graph of DNA molecules from which linked reads are sequenced, excluding repetitive stretches. Then it deconvolutes vertices that represent multiple molecules (due to barcode reuse in linked reads) using a novel community detection algorithm. Finally, it calculates a representative path through the graph as the physical map. In the scaffolding stage, it maps the input assembly to the physical map using minimizers and uses ARCS to order and orient pieces.
Strengths_weaknesses: Builds chromosome-level physical maps of the sequencing data. Improves draft assemblies of various technologies including long-reads assemblies using linked-read data
Overall_performance: Requires a draft assembly (with fragments of length > 100kb) to succeed.

SneakySnake:snake: is the first and the only pre-alignment filtering algorithm that works efficiently and fast on modern CPU, FPGA, and GPU architectures. It greatly (by more than two orders of magnitude) expedites sequence alignment calculation for both short and long reads.
Underlying_algorithms: The key idea of SneakySnake is to reduce the approximate string matching (ASM) problem to the single net routing (SNR) problem in VLSI chip layout. In the SNR problem, we are interested in only finding the optimal path that connects two terminals with the least routing cost on a special grid layout that contains obstacles. The SneakySnake algorithm quickly solves the SNR problem and uses the found optimal path to decide whether performing sequence alignment is necessary. Reducing the ASM problem into SNR also makes SneakySnake efficient to implement for modern high-performance computing architectures (CPUs, GPUs, and FPGAs).
Overall_performance: SneakySnake significantly improves the accuracy of pre-alignment filtering by up to four orders of magnitude compared to the state-of-the-art pre-alignment filters, Shouji, GateKeeper, and SHD. Using short sequences, SneakySnake accelerates Edlib (state-of-the-art implementation of Myers bit-vector algorithm) and Parasail (sequence aligner with configurable scoring function), by up to 37.7x and 43.9x (>12x on average), respectively, without requiring hardware acceleration, and by up to 413x and 689x (>400x on average), respectively, using hardware acceleration. Using long sequences, SneakySnake accelerates Parasail by up to 979x (140.1x on average). SneakySnake also accelerates the sequence alignment of minimap2, a state-of-the-art read mapper, by up to 6.83x (4.67x on average) and 64.1x (17.1x on average) using short and long sequences, respectively, and without requiring hardware acceleration. As SneakySnake does not replace sequence alignment, users can still configure the aligner of their choice for different scoring functions, surpassing most existing efforts that aim to accelerate sequence alignment.

Underlying_algorithms: minimap2; hifiasm; DeepVariant; GLNexus; Sniffles; Severus; HiFiCNV; TRGT; STRdust; HiPhase; WhatsHap; LongPhase; modkit; pb-CpG-tools; VEP; GENMOD
Underlying_assumptions: Nextflow-based workflow following nf-core conventions; requires genome reference and BAM/FASTQ long-read input
Strengths_weaknesses: Comprehensive rare-disease long-read pipeline; integrates many best-practice sub-tools; suitable for clinical genomics

Details: VADA-NF (Variational Autoregressive DNA-conditioned Autoencoder Normalizing Flow) is a data-driven simulator for nanopore sequencing. An improvement over the original VADA model, it uses a generative model to produce realistic nanopore signals conditioned on DNA sequence, enabling accurate benchmarking of basecallers and downstream tools.
Underlying_algorithms: Variational autoregressive latent variable model; normalizing flow; DNA-conditioned signal generation
Underlying_assumptions: Requires real nanopore signal training data to learn the simulator model; simulates raw current signals conditioned on DNA sequence

Details: A machine learning-based tool for A/Z (2,6-diaminopurine / Z-base) base calling and dZ-DNA detection using PacBio HiFi reads. Identifies dZ-DNA molecules and discriminates between adenine (A) and 2,6-diaminopurine (Z) bases by analyzing sequence context-dependent kinetic signal changes in HiFi CCS reads. Also includes a pipeline for taxonomic/source detection of dZ-DNAs in mixed datasets.
Underlying_algorithms: Sequence context-dependent kinetic signal analysis; machine learning classification of A vs Z bases
Underlying_assumptions: Requires PacBio HiFi (CCS) reads with interpulse duration (IPD) kinetics; assumes Z-base substitutions alter kinetic signatures

Details: An open-source computational pipeline for processing single-cell long-read RNA sequencing data from the BenchDrop-seq platform (a microfluidics-free benchtop method for single-cell partitioning and molecular barcoding using Oxford Nanopore sequencing). Handles FASTQ reads with cellular barcodes and UMIs; outputs transcript- and gene-level count matrices, isoform equivalence classes, and BAM alignment files.
Underlying_algorithms: Barcode recovery; splice-aware alignment; UMI deduplication; transcript and gene quantification
Underlying_assumptions: Requires BenchDrop-seq barcoded long reads in FASTQ format; designed for 10x-style cell barcodes with long-read ONT data

Details: Chemistry-agnostic, feature-first software package for annotation and reconstruction of full-length transcripts from long-read RNA sequencing data.

Details: bloodTyper is an automated interpretive software designed for targeted Next Generation Sequencing (NGS) ABO genotyping. It enables reliable genotyping of ABO allele haplotypes without subject matter expert intervention.
Strengths_weaknesses: Strengths: Enables reliable genotyping of ABO allele haplotypes without expert intervention, improves characterization of the ABO gene, and can uncover important changes not initially found by previous workups. Weaknesses: Not specified, but the paper addresses complexities of serologic testing that genotyping aims to overcome.
Overall_performance: The targeted NGS results analyzed by bloodTyper showed 99.3% concordance (n=287/289) with serology for solid organ and stem cell donor samples. It successfully phased three samples tested with long-read sequencing.

Details: Fedrann is an open-source tool for effective long-read overlap detection in de novo genome assembly pipelines. It utilizes a novel strategy integrating feature extraction, dimensionality reduction, and approximate nearest neighbor search to handle large-scale datasets and repetitive genomic regions.
Underlying_algorithms: Feature extraction, dimensionality reduction (Sparse Random Projection - SRP), approximate nearest neighbor (ANN) search (NNDescent), inverse document frequency (IDF) transformation.
Strengths_weaknesses: Strengths: Accurate overlap detection across diverse datasets, efficient, competitive runtime, generates comparable or better overlap graphs than state-of-the-art tools, robust and scalable. Achieves performance parity with tools in compiled languages through C-accelerated libraries. Weaknesses: Not explicitly stated.
Overall_performance: Produces overlap graphs comparable to or better than MECAT2, minimap2, and wtdbg2 with competitive runtime. Achieves high assembly contiguity and quality when integrated into the Shasta assembler for human whole genomes.

Details: AgileStructure is a desktop application designed for locating and annotating large-scale genomic rearrangements using aligned long-read data. It enables user-guided exploration of breakpoint-spanning reads to support accurate interpretation of complex structural variants.
Strengths_weaknesses: Strength: Addresses the limitations of short-read sequencing in resolving breakpoints of large rearrangements, especially in repetitive or low-complexity regions, by enabling accurate identification and annotation of large structural variants with long-read data. Weakness: Relies on pre-aligned long-read data.
